item.title: Don't miss the spring blossoms, philosophers! Do you think beauty is magnified b...
item.description:
@philosophybreak

Don't miss the spring blossoms, philosophers!

Do you think beauty is magnified by impermanence? What examples do you see in art, culture, and nature? If the basic condition of existence is change, does that mean happiness requires us to internalize and embrace impermanence? Or can we live good lives even while railing against the transience of all we hold dear?

If you enjoy insights from philosophy, you might like my short Sunday breakdown. I distill one famous philosophical idea; you get the summary straight to your inbox. Consider joining 25,000+ thinkers enjoying a nugget of profundity every Sunday by signing up for free via the link in my bio (one-click sign up, no spam, unsubscribe any time) 👉 @philosophybreak 💭

#philosophy #mononoaware #impermanence
item.content:encoded:
@philosophybreak

Don't miss the spring blossoms, philosophers!

Do you think beauty is magnified by impermanence? What examples do you see in art, culture, and nature? If the basic condition of existence is change, does that mean happiness requires us to internalize and embrace impermanence? Or can we live good lives even while railing against the transience of all we hold dear?

If you enjoy insights from philosophy, you might like my short Sunday breakdown. I distill one famous philosophical idea; you get the summary straight to your inbox. Consider joining 25,000+ thinkers enjoying a nugget of profundity every Sunday by signing up for free via the link in my bio (one-click sign up, no spam, unsubscribe any time) 👉 @philosophybreak 💭

#philosophy #mononoaware #impermanence
item.author: philosophybreak

item.title: Plath’s evocation, taken from her haunting 1963 novel The Bell Jar, might bring ...
item.description:
@philosophybreak

Plath’s evocation, taken from her haunting 1963 novel The Bell Jar, might bring to mind the warnings behind Kierkegaard’s “dizziness of freedom”, and Sartre’s comment that “Man is condemned to be free. Condemned, because he did not create himself; because, once hurled into the world, he is responsible for everything he does.” To see how Kierkegaard and Sartre think we can reconcile ourselves to existential freedom, you might like my Existence Precedes Essence on Philosophy Break.

#philosophy #sylviaplath #decisionmaking
item.content:encoded:
@philosophybreak

Plath’s evocation, taken from her haunting 1963 novel The Bell Jar, might bring to mind the warnings behind Kierkegaard’s “dizziness of freedom”, and Sartre’s comment that “Man is condemned to be free. Condemned, because he did not create himself; because, once hurled into the world, he is responsible for everything he does.” To see how Kierkegaard and Sartre think we can reconcile ourselves to existential freedom, you might like my Existence Precedes Essence on Philosophy Break.

#philosophy #sylviaplath #decisionmaking
item.author: philosophybreak

item.title: My name’s Jack, I live in London, and with Philosophy Break I’m aiming to do two...
item.description:
@philosophybreak

My name’s Jack, I live in London, and with Philosophy Break I’m aiming to do two things for you:

1. Help you quickly understand the most powerful, mind-opening ideas in philosophy.

2. Offer clear next steps for when you want to further explore a particular philosopher or topic.

Having spent 15+ years studying philosophy (picking up a master’s degree along the way), my aim is to distill everything I’ve learned (and continue to learn) from my meandering philosophical education into ultra-concise materials, available to you here and beyond.

My hope is to provide shortcuts to the ‘good stuff’, and offer clear direction for when you want to dive deeper.

I do this through social media, but primarily through my website and newsletter at philosophybreak.com, where I distill & discuss one famous philosophical idea per week.

Thank you very much for reading and engaging with Philosophy Break.

If you’ve enjoyed my content here, I think you’ll get a lot out of the newsletter. It’s totally free, and you can join thousands of like-minded thinkers who discuss philosophy each week. I love hearing from you, and read and respond to every message.

Simply hit the link in my bio to sign up 👉 @philosophybreak 💭⁣⁣⁣⁣⁣

#philosophy #philosophystudent #philosophymajor
item.content:encoded:
@philosophybreak

My name’s Jack, I live in London, and with Philosophy Break I’m aiming to do two things for you:

1. Help you quickly understand the most powerful, mind-opening ideas in philosophy.

2. Offer clear next steps for when you want to further explore a particular philosopher or topic.

Having spent 15+ years studying philosophy (picking up a master’s degree along the way), my aim is to distill everything I’ve learned (and continue to learn) from my meandering philosophical education into ultra-concise materials, available to you here and beyond.

My hope is to provide shortcuts to the ‘good stuff’, and offer clear direction for when you want to dive deeper.

I do this through social media, but primarily through my website and newsletter at philosophybreak.com, where I distill & discuss one famous philosophical idea per week.

Thank you very much for reading and engaging with Philosophy Break.

If you’ve enjoyed my content here, I think you’ll get a lot out of the newsletter. It’s totally free, and you can join thousands of like-minded thinkers who discuss philosophy each week. I love hearing from you, and read and respond to every message.

Simply hit the link in my bio to sign up 👉 @philosophybreak 💭⁣⁣⁣⁣⁣

#philosophy #philosophystudent #philosophymajor
